interrogator_dossier:
# YELP — Interrogator Forensic Dossier

*Inputs: eight quarters of Yelp earnings-call transcripts (Q2-2024 → Q1-2026) cross-referenced against the screener's actual financial trajectory (FY2021–FY2025 + TTM/Q1-2026). Signal type: deep_value. Live catalyst checks dated to June 2026.*

---

## 1. NARRATIVE ARC

Yelp's story over these eight quarters is a slow-motion **pivot from "disciplined profitable-growth ad platform" to "AI transformation play,"** forced by the steady erosion of half its revenue base.

- **Q2-2024 → Q4-2024 (the "two-speed" story).** The opening narrative is clean and bifurcated: *Services* is the engine ("13th consecutive quarter of double-digit growth," Q2-24; reaching 15 consecutive quarters by Q4-24), while *Restaurants, Retail & Other* (RR&O) is the anchor dragging on the boat ("RR&O revenue declined 3%… challenging operating environment," Q2-24). Management's posture is *offensive on discipline*: record net revenue, expanding margins, and an explicit promise to cut SBC "to less than 8% of revenue by end of 2025." Capital return is front-and-center — $63M, $62.5M, $62.5M of buybacks per quarter. The one new growth lever introduced is **paid search / paid project acquisition**, pitched aggressively in Q2 ($35M planned) then quietly walked back by Q3-24 ("not sufficient to warrant continued investment at the current level").

- **Q3-2024 (the first acquisition pivot).** RepairPal ($80M cash, ~$30M revenue, breakeven) is announced — the first sign management will *buy* growth in adjacent services (auto) rather than generate it organically. The paid-search experiment is being dialed down even as they announce it worked on top-of-funnel metrics. This is the first "we tried X, it didn't clear our ROI bar, so we're buying Y instead" rotation.

- **Q4-2024 / FY-2024 (peak confidence).** The strongest quarter of the window: net income +34% YoY to $133M, EPS +40% to $1.88, fully-diluted share count down materially. Management adds a *second* SBC target ("less than 6% of revenue by end of 2027") and frames AI as an *accelerant* of the existing three pillars (lead in services, drive advertiser value, transform consumer experience).

- **Q4-2025 / FY-2025 (the narrative inflection).** The story visibly **broadens away from the core ad business** because the core is cracking: RR&O now -6% YoY to $444M, total ad clicks -7%, app unique devices -2%. To compensate, management foregrounds **three brand-new AI/SaaS legs**: data licensing (the **OpenAI deal**), Yelp Host (AI call-answering, "190,000 calls"), and the **Hatch acquisition** (AI lead management, "70% YoY growth"). "Other revenue" is suddenly a hero line (+17% YoY). The 5-year buyback flex appears: fully-diluted share count cut from 86M to 67M (-22%) since 2021.

- **Q1-2026 (full pivot, growth stall).** The transformation is now the *entire* story. Total revenue +1% YoY — effectively flat. RR&O ad revenue collapses **-11% YoY**, Services ad revenue decelerates to **+1%**, adjusted-EBITDA margin compresses to **22%** (from 26-28% through 2024), net income -27% YoY. The only growth is "other revenue" +75% (Hatch consolidation + licensing + DoorDash food ordering +88%). Management introduces a *fresh* multi-year target — **$250M other-revenue run-rate by end-2028 (>2x Q1-26)** — and leans hard on an AI-productivity margin story ("a significant portion of our code… is AI generated," "migrations… three months to three weeks").

**Net arc:** the growth drivers introduced at the start (Services double-digit growth, paid search) have decelerated or been dropped; the drivers carrying the story now (licensing, Hatch, Host) did not exist 18 months ago and are a rounding error on revenue. The thesis has *expanded in ambition and contracted in proof.*

---

## 2. CLAIMS vs FINANCIALS (forensic)

| Mgmt claim | Quarter(s) | Actual financials | Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|
| "Record net revenue… strong profitability" | every quarter | Revenue did set records through 2025 ($1.03B→$1.19B→$1.34B→$1.41B→$1.46B), but **3-yr CAGR is only +7.1% and decelerating to +3.7% then ~+1%** | **Partly true but flattering** — "record" masks a sharp deceleration |
| "Services… double-digit growth" (15+ consecutive qtrs) | Q2-24→Q4-25 | True through 2025 (+11% to $948M). But **Q1-26 Services ad revenue grew only +1% YoY** — the engine just stalled | **TRUE then BROKEN.** The headline streak ended in the most recent quarter |
| "Disciplined expense management… expanding margins" | Q2-24→Q4-24 | Confirmed: net margin 9.9%, FCF margin 22%, EBITDA margin 26-28% through 2024 | **TRUE for 2024** |
| Margin discipline (forward) | Q1-26 | **EBITDA margin fell to 22%; net income -27% YoY; net margin to 5% in the quarter.** Margins are now *compressing*, not expanding | **DIVERGES** — narrative still says "margin potential," numbers say compression |
| EPS / earnings power growing | FY24, FY25 | EPS $1.35→$1.88→$2.24 (5-yr EPS CAGR +45.5%) — but **almost entirely buyback-driven**: NI grew 99→133→146M (much slower) while share count shrank 22% | **TRUE but engineered** — EPS growth ≫ net-income growth = financial engineering, not operating leverage |
| FCF strength | all | Screener FY FCF margin 22%; **TTM FCF $281M** (≈19% of revenue) on a $1.6B market cap = ~4.7x P/FCF | **TRUE and the core of the value case** — this is real, high-quality cash generation |
| "Returning excess capital… healthy cash balance" | all | **Q1-26 reality: cash drawn from ~$320M to $110M, all marketable securities sold, $130M now drawn on the credit facility.** Net cash is gone | **DIVERGES from the "fortress balance sheet" prior** — they are now levering modestly to keep buying back stock + fund Hatch |
| RR&O is a temporary macro problem ("in-restaurant dining will return") | Q4-25, Q1-26 | RR&O has declined **every single quarter for ~2.5 years**: -3%→-6%→-6%→-3%→…→-11%. The decline is *accelerating*, not bottoming | **NARRATIVE POORER THAN ADMITTED** — management frames it as cyclical; the trajectory looks secular (AI/Google disintermediation of local search) |
| AI / licensing / SaaS = the next growth leg | Q4-25, Q1-26 | "Other revenue" $29M in Q1-26 (≈8% of revenue), targeted to $250M run-rate by 2028. Even if hit, that is ~$60M/yr today vs a ~$1.4B ad base shrinking ~mid-single-digits | **UNPROVEN / TOO SMALL TO MOVE THE NEEDLE YET** — the math doesn't offset RR&O erosion for years |
| SBC reduction (<8% by end-2025, <6% by end-2027) | all | Confirmed on track: hit 8% of revenue in Q1-26, down 2pp YoY | **CREDIBLE — delivered** |

**Bottom line:** the *value* claims (cheap, FCF-rich, buying back stock aggressively, real margins) are **corroborated by the financials**. The *growth* claims (Services strength, margin expansion, AI re-acceleration) have either **just broken (Services +1%, EBITDA 22%)** or are **too embryonic to verify (other revenue).** The story is richer than the numbers on the growth side and roughly honest on the value side.

---

## 3. TONE & CONFIDENCE TRAJECTORY

**Trajectory: DECLINING (offensive → defensive).**

- **Q2-2024 — Confident/offensive.** "We were pleased with the continued progress… we remain confident in our strategy to drive profitable growth and shareholder value." Raising EBITDA guide $10M at the midpoint despite RR&O. Spending aggressively on paid search.
- **Q3-2024 — Confident, pivoting.** Still raising; announcing an acquisition from a position of strength ("demonstrates our ability to deploy capital from our balance sheet").
- **Q4-2024 — Peak confidence.** "Record… 34% net income growth… 40% EPS growth." Adds a second multi-year SBC target — the body language of a team that feels in control.
- **Q4-2025 — Defensive pivot begins.** Tone shifts to *justification*: "the operating environment for RR&O categories remain challenging." The emphasis migrates from "growth" to "transformation," "trusted content," "essential partner" — aspirational framing that substitutes narrative for near-term numbers.
- **Q1-2026 — Defensive / damage-control.** Revenue +1%, EBITDA margin 22%, NI -27%. The language pivots hard to **cost/efficiency and the future**: "substantial opportunities to unlock operational efficiencies… with AI," "code… is AI generated," and a brand-new 2028 target to give investors something to hold onto. CFO repeatedly invokes "the degree of uncertainty… variability… the conflict in the Middle East" — classic externalization of a deteriorating result.

**On guidance specificity:** management actually became *more* generous with multi-year targets as results weakened (the 2028 other-revenue target is new in Q1-26) — a recognizable pattern of *lengthening the horizon* when the near term disappoints.

---

## 4. GUIDANCE CREDIBILITY

**Reliability: MEDIUM-HIGH on the metrics they control; LOW on the trajectory they don't.**

- **Beat rate:** screener notes **7/7 quarters beat (100%)**, and the transcripts corroborate a consistent sandbagging pattern — Q1-26 adjusted EBITDA came in **$15M above the high end** of the outlook range (and the same "$14-16M above the high end" beat recurs in Q2-24, Q3-24, Q4-24). This is a **structural sandbag**: they guide EBITDA conservatively and flow outperformance through. The CFO essentially admits the mechanism in Q1-26: "as we've been able to outperform our guidance, we've flowed through that incremental revenue to EBITDA."
- **But the *direction* of guidance has been serially cut.** Full-year revenue was guided down in Q2-24 (-$12.5M), again in Q3-24 (-$18M at midpoint), and the multi-year RR&O recovery they expected ("better performance in RR&O in Q4," Q3-24) never came. They reliably **beat a bar they keep lowering.**
- **The SBC commitments have been delivered** (<8% hit on schedule), which is a genuine credibility marker.

**Net:** management is *reliable on near-term EBITDA prints and capital-return execution*, and *unreliable as a forecaster of its own top line* — every forward RR&O call has been too optimistic. Score: **Medium-High execution credibility, Low forecasting credibility.** For a deep-value/buyback thesis, the execution credibility is what matters more.

---

## 5. ANALYST PRESSURE POINTS

The Q&A reveals where the bodies are buried — and analysts have been circling the same three holes for two years:

1. **Google / AI-search disintermediation of traffic (the existential question).** Repeatedly probed: Dion Lee (Evercore, Q2-24) on AI-search conversion; Kishan Patel (Raymond James, Q1-25) on Perplexity and "search results ranking notably different"; Nitin Bansal (BofA, Q1-25) on "Google ramping AI overviews… how that is impacting your web traffic." Management's answer is *consistently a non-answer*: "haven't seen any material or unusual shifts," "too early," "a space to watch." Then in **Q1-26 the shoe drops** — Nitin Bansal points out the OpenAI partnership is hollow in practice: *"when I search for restaurants on ChatGPT today, Yelp content appears relatively limited versus Reddit, OpenTable, Tripadvisor."* Stoppelman cannot answer: "we can't really comment on… their plans." **The thing analysts feared (traffic erosion / failure to convert AI partnerships into visibility) is now visible in -7% ad clicks and -2% devices.**

2. **RR&O — is it cyclical or secular?** Pushed every quarter (John Colantuoni/Jefferies, Q3-24, "dig into the pressure within RR&O"; Kishan Patel, Q4-25, "what needs to change for that advertiser base to stabilize"). Management insists "in-restaurant dining will return" and blames "a weakened consumer… high input costs." They have *never* engaged with the possibility that local restaurants are structurally moving spend to Google/Instagram/DoorDash. **Two-and-a-half years of uninterrupted decline argues the analysts' unspoken secular worry is right.**

3. **The economics of the new bets (paid search, then Hatch/Host).** Analysts pressed hard on paid-search ROI in 2024 — and management *abandoned it* after spending ~$54M, validating the skepticism. Now the same scrutiny lands on Hatch ("margin trajectory goals," Chris/Jefferies Q3-24; Kishan Patel Q4-25) and Yelp Host. Management's answer — "driving margin is not the immediate focus… we want to realize the opportunity" — is the *exact* framing they used for paid search before pulling the plug. **The pattern of "invest aggressively → declare it didn't clear the ROI bar → rotate to the next thing" is the single biggest tell that the growth investments may not compound.**

---

## 6. RED FLAGS / GREEN FLAGS

🔴 **Services growth engine stalled to +1% (Q1-26)** — the 15-quarter double-digit streak that *was the entire bull case* on the operating side just ended. This is the most important datapoint in the file.

🔴 **RR&O decline accelerating, not bottoming** (-11% in Q1-26, the worst print of the window) after 2.5 years — increasingly looks secular (AI/Google taking local-search intent), not cyclical.

🔴 **Margin compression** — adjusted EBITDA margin 26-28% (2024) → 25% (FY25) → **22% (Q1-26)**; net income -27% YoY. The "disciplined margin expansion" story has reversed.

🔴 **Balance-sheet cushion drained** — cash from ~$320M to $110M in one quarter, *all* marketable securities sold, **$130M now drawn on the credit facility.** The historic net-cash fortress (a pillar of the value case) is effectively gone; they are levering to fund buybacks + Hatch.

🔴 **Insider conviction is negative** — open-market reality: **0 buys vs 39 sells (528,383 shares).** The screener's grant-inflated "buy ratio 0.87" is noise; the real signal is one-directional selling.

🔴 **Repeating "invest → abandon → rotate" pattern** — paid search (~$54M spent, dropped); risk that Hatch/Host follow.

🔴 **AI partnerships are PR-rich, substance-thin** — the OpenAI deal does not yet surface Yelp meaningfully in ChatGPT (per BofA's own check, Q1-26); management can't quantify any traffic/revenue benefit.

🟢 **Buyback execution is real and aggressive** — fully-diluted share count **86M → 67M (-22%) over four years**; ~$292M repurchased in 2025; this is the genuine engine converting flat net income into +19-40% EPS growth.

🟢 **Cash generation is high-quality and the valuation is genuinely cheap** — TTM FCF $281M, ~4.7x P/FCF, 90.3% gross margin. Even in decline, this throws off a ~19-22% FCF yield to the EV.

🟢 **SBC reduction delivered on schedule** (<8% of revenue, Q1-26) — improves the *quality* of EBITDA and is a rare kept multi-year promise.

🟢 **"Other revenue" inflecting** (+75% YoY Q1-26; food ordering +88%; Hatch run-rate $34M, +92%) — small but a real second engine forming, with a credible (if unproven) $250M-by-2028 target.

🟢 **Governance signal** — appointment of **Logan Green (Lyft co-founder)** to the board (Aug-2025) and to the Nominating Committee — a credible operator added during a strategic transition.

---

## 7. HIDDEN SIGNALS

- **Terminology migration is the loudest tell.** Across the window the lexicon shifts from *"profitable growth / margin leverage / disciplined"* (2024) to *"transform / AI / essential partner / trusted content / answers and actions / SaaS / TAM"* (2025-26). When a company's vocabulary moves from financial metrics to platform-vision adjectives, the metrics are usually deteriorating — and here they are.

- **The CFO quietly reframes the buyback rationale.** Early calls describe a *three-part* capital-allocation policy ("healthy cash balance, capacity for acquisitions, return excess capital"). By Q1-26 the cash balance has been gutted and they've *borrowed* to keep returning capital. The policy language stayed constant while the underlying posture flipped from self-funded to credit-funded — an unflagged change in financial risk appetite.

- **"Excluding projects acquired through our paid search initiative…"** (Q4-25). Management began *carving out* paid-search-driven projects from the Request-a-Quote growth number — an admission that the headline project-growth figure was partly bought, not organic. Reported RAQ growth was "~25%," but *organic* was ~15%.

- **The 2028 target as a confidence prosthesis.** Introducing a fresh, distant (2028) other-revenue target *in the exact quarter* the core stalled (+1% revenue) is a recognizable move to redirect investor attention past a weak present toward an unfalsifiable future.

- **Logan Green's arrival + a depleted balance sheet + a stock at multi-year lows** is the kind of setup that *attracts* activists. The 2023 TCS Capital campaign (which sought a $70+ sale) faded, but the structural ingredients (sum-of-parts discount, fortress-business economics, no controlling shareholder, cheap stock) remain — this is a latent, not active, governance catalyst.

---

## 8. CAPITAL-ALLOCATION VERDICT (for the director)

**Classification: PIVOTING — a high-quality, cash-generative franchise whose core ad engine is eroding (Services just stalled to +1%, RR&O -11%), now mid-pivot into still-tiny AI/SaaS/licensing lines, while management compounds per-share value through an aggressive, now credit-funded buyback.**

The single most important thing the director must weigh before sizing: **this is a buyback-compounder value thesis, not a catalyst/event thesis.** The return engine is purely *capital allocation* — 22% share-count reduction converting a flat-to-declining ~$280M FCF stream into rising per-share value at ~4.7x P/FCF. There is **no hard-dated forcing function**: the OpenAI/Hatch/Host catalysts are real but immaterial and unproven, and the only event-driver with re-rating power (activist push for a sale) is *latent, not active* (TCS faded in 2023-24; nothing live as of June 2026).

The bear risk that could break even the value case: **if Services-ad and RR&O decline together drag total revenue negative and FCF rolls over faster than the share count shrinks, the buyback is just slowing a melting ice cube** — and the freshly-drawn credit line means the balance-sheet shock absorber is thinner than it was a year ago. Size it as a *cheap, well-managed, secularly-challenged cash compounder with optional latent-activist upside* — not as a special situation with a clock.

---

CREDIBILITY_SCORE: 3 | TRAJECTORY: PIVOTING

bull_thesis: Yelp is a genuine free-cash-flow machine trading at 4.7x P/FCF (TTM FCF $281M) with 90.3% gross margins, a clean balance sheet (net debt only ~$45M), and the strongest pro-shareholder capital allocation in its peer set: management has retired 22% of the share count (86M -> 67M, 2021-2025) and bought back $125M in Q1-2026 alone at $24.58 -- i.e., aggressively repurchasing into the stock near 52-week lows, with $414M of authorization remaining. At a ~$23 price the market is pricing in substantial, accelerating revenue erosion that has not actually happened: FY2025 revenue still grew +4% and FCF +30% YoY. The bull case is that the core ad business STABILIZES at a lower volume rather than collapsing -- Services ad revenue grew to a record $948M (+8%) in FY25 and the RR&O decline is cyclical/macro (weak consumer, restaurant input-cost squeeze) that laps and recovers. Layered on top is real, fast-growing optionality: Other revenue +75% YoY in Q1-26 to $29M, with Hatch ($34M ARR, +92%), Yelp Host (1.5M-call annual run-rate by April 2026, $1B+ stated US TAM), DoorDash food ordering (+88%), and a genuinely scarce data-licensing asset (330M reviews, 8M listings) that OpenAI, Apple Maps, Bing, Alexa and Meta.ai are now paying to license. Management is targeting $250M other-revenue run-rate by end-2028 (>2x current). With SBC falling to 8% of revenue (target <6% by 2027) improving GAAP quality, and a buyback shrinking the float ~10%+/year, per-share FCF compounds even on a flat top line. The forensic record supports the cash claims: 7/7 EPS beats, EBITDA consistently $11-15M above the high end of guidance. If the core merely flattens, the buyback alone re-rates the equity toward the $50+ intrinsic-value band.
bear_thesis: The cheapness is the symptom of a structurally shrinking franchise, not a mispricing. The core advertising engine is in slow secular decline: paying advertising locations have fallen EVERY quarter in the window (517k -> 515k -> 512k -> ... -> 485k, -6% YoY in Q1-26), ad clicks have been negative all six quarters and are accelerating downward (-3% -> -7% -> -11% -> -10%), app unique devices are -2% YoY, and consumers are visiting 'with reduced frequency.' Critically, the one durable growth engine -- Services -- has now ALSO stalled to +1% ad-revenue growth in Q1-26, so the deceleration is no longer just RR&O macro. Headline revenue growth collapsed from +8% (Q1-25) to +1% (Q1-26); net income fell -27% YoY and adjusted EBITDA -7% in Q1-26 as the pivot starts costing current profitability. The rising-CPC/falling-clicks dynamic is the signature of monetizing a DECLINING audience harder, not a healthy platform. The secular threat is material-to-terminal: Google Maps and Meta already own the top of the local-discovery funnel, and generative-AI search (ChatGPT, Gemini) now threatens to disintermediate Yelp's consumer destination entirely -- management ADMITTED on the Q1-26 call it cannot control how OpenAI surfaces its content and that Yelp appears 'relatively limited versus Reddit, OpenTable, Tripadvisor' in ChatGPT today. Yelp's counter -- license the corpus to the disruptors -- monetizes the data but cedes the consumer relationship and caps the economics at a licensing fee, not ad-network rent. Insider behavior confirms the skepticism: 0 open-market buys vs 33 sells (476,663 shares). ROIC is only 9.0% and falling -- an eroding moat. The $250M-by-2028 other-revenue target is replacing, not adding to, a melting ad base; 'records' and 'transformation' rhetoric mask a +1% business. If RR&O and Services keep declining and AI-search accelerates audience leakage, the buyback is just running down the clock on a value trap.
consensus_delta: For a deep_value name the question is: 'Is the street pricing terminal decline into a business with stable/growing earnings power?' Partly. The street's analyst target of $27.50 (+18%) and target-velocity -11% show consensus has given up on growth and is anchoring to a melting-ad-base narrative -- that is RIGHT about RR&O (-11% YoY) and increasingly right about Services (now +1%, the deceleration the bulls said wouldn't happen). Where consensus is arguably WRONG: (1) it under-weights the per-share compounding from a buyback retiring ~10%/yr of the float at <5x FCF -- even flat revenue produces rising per-share FCF; (2) it gives near-zero credit to the data-licensing asset that OpenAI/Apple/Bing/Meta are demonstrably paying for, and to Other revenue compounding +75% toward a $250M/2028 target. The expectations-arb is therefore NOT the classic 'street thinks it's dying, it's actually growing' -- the core really is shrinking. It is the subtler 'street prices the equity as if the cash and the buyback don't exist.' That is a real but lower-octane edge, and it is contaminated by the genuine AI-displacement tail, which is why the value_conviction is capped at 3 (an eroding moat + material-secular base).
forcing_function: There is NO hard-dated re-rating catalyst. The TCS Capital activist sale-push dates to 2023 and is dormant -- no live strategic review or process exists as of June 2026 (verified: no sale process, no takeover offer; Hatch was Yelp acquiring, not being acquired). The Hatch deal already CLOSED (Feb 2026, ~$270-300M cash) -- that re-rate is spent. The only genuine forcing function is the slow-grind BUYBACK ($414M remaining authorization, ~10%/yr float reduction) compounding per-share value, plus the next earnings prints (Q2-2026 ~early Aug, Q3 ~early Nov) which will either confirm core stabilization (bullish re-rate trigger) or confirm Services decline (bearish). The 2028 $250M other-revenue target is a multi-quarter proof-point, not a date. This is a SOFT_EXTENDED catalyst profile: real value, real buyback, but no binding dated event to force consensus -- the thesis is a patience/compounding trade, not an event trade.
valley_of_death: Near-term (3-9 month) risks are real but not balance-sheet-threatening. (1) Continued top-line deceleration: Services has stalled to +1% and RR&O is -11%; Q2/Q3-2026 prints could show total revenue flat-to-down YoY, which would shatter the 'stabilization' leg of the bull case and re-rate fair value toward the bear band. (2) Margin compression as the pivot spends in: Q1-26 net income already -27% YoY and adj EBITDA -7%; Hatch is being run for growth not margin (retention payments added back to EBITDA -- a quality-of-earnings drag), and capitalized software is flattering adjusted EBITDA. (3) Macro: management explicitly flagged 'the conflict in the Middle East' and a weak local-business consumer -- consistent with the regime's live oil/inflation tail -- as a Q1-26 headwind that may persist. There is NO debt maturity wall or cash-burn hump (net debt only ~$45M, TTM FCF $281M positive), so the 'valley' is a fundamental-deterioration / multiple-stays-low risk, not a solvency event. The buyback is the shock absorber.
positioning_washout: Limited mechanical-selling dynamic -- this is not a yield/dividend name (no dividend to cut) and not heavily passive-owned in a way that forces liquidation. Smart-money score 35/100 and institutional flow 60/100 suggest a mixed, not crowded-long, base. The cleaner positioning signal is on the OTHER side: insiders are net sellers (0 open-market buys vs 33 sells, 476,663 shares), and the company itself is the marginal buyer via the buyback. So rather than a washout of weak hands setting up a bounce, the setup is a steady company-bid (buyback) absorbing insider and passive supply. A capitulation flush would more likely come from a single bad print (a Services NEGATIVE quarter or an AI-search disclosure that audience is leaking) than from forced mechanical selling. That is the event to WAIT for.
moderator_conclusion: YELP is a real free-cash-flow machine (4.7x P/FCF, 90% gross margin, ~$45M net debt, TTM FCF $281M) with best-in-cohort capital return -- a 22% share-count reduction and $125M of Q1-26 buyback at $24.58 near the lows. That alone puts a hard floor near the current ~$23 and gives a favorable headline risk/reward (~5-15% downside-to-break vs ~+74% to base-case $40 SoP). BUT the catalyst is SOFT_EXTENDED, not hard-dated: the TCS activism is dormant (2023, no live process), Hatch's re-rate is FIRED (closed Feb-2026), and the only live forcing function is the gradual buyback plus the next two earnings prints. Applying the June-2026 regime (CATALYST_WATCH_REGIME.md): the regime rewards HARD-DATED structural special-sits in thin lanes and PENALIZES theses that need stabilization/macro to rescue them -- YELP is exactly the latter (it needs the local-ad core to stop shrinking and needs the AI-search tail not to bite). The regime also flags AI-capex/AI-displacement as the dominant shared factor and demands resolution-driver independence; YELP's whole bear case IS the AI-displacement tail, so it offers no independence and concentrates the book's #1 secular risk. Net: this is a legitimate cheap, well-managed, buyback-floored cash compounder, but with an ERODING moat (ROIC 9% and falling, audience -2%, paying locations -6%), a MATERIAL secular threat (Google Maps/Meta + generative-AI search disintermediating local discovery -- management itself admits it can't control how ChatGPT surfaces its content), and no binding catalyst. Verdict B (WATCHLIST FOR CAPITULATION), conviction 3: wait for a specific flush -- a Services-NEGATIVE quarter or an audience-leakage disclosure -- to buy the cash machine at a price where the secular risk is fully discounted; do not pay up for a stabilization re-rate that the Q1-26 Services stall is actively undermining. value_conviction also 3: the value case judged on its own is genuine (deep FCF discount, fortress buyback) but is CAPPED at 3 by the eroding moat + material AI-displacement secular threat -- a low multiple on a structurally-shrinking local-ad base is value-trap-adjacent, not clean value.
sop_breakdown: Reconciled base-case ~ $40/share. The ~22%-historical / ~10%/yr ongoing buyback is the load-bearing value lever that gets price toward this band even with flat revenue.
risk_reward: At ~$23: downside-to-break ~$20-24 (the adverse SoP / FCF-multiple floor, where the buyback caps it) = ~5-15% downside; upside-to-fair-value ~$40 base / ~$50 favorable = ~+74% to +117%. Headline R:R is attractive (~1:5 to break vs fair) BECAUSE the buyback creates a hard floor near current levels -- but the asymmetry is contingent on the core merely stabilizing; if AI-search disintermediation accelerates, fair value itself migrates down toward the bear $20-26 and the 'discount' evaporates. Net: favorable skew, but the upside is a re-rating bet on stabilization, not a dated catalyst.
peer_comps_note: Peer set ANGI/TRIP/GOOGL/META/IAC/MAPS/CARG; verdict cheap_vs_peers. At 4.7x P/FCF Yelp is dramatically the cheapest local-advertising platform (TRIP ~10-15x FCF; ANGI FCF-negative). The 90.3% gross margin is software-like. HOWEVER, per the peer file's own rationale, the discount is PARTIALLY JUSTIFIED by structural moat erosion -- Google Maps and Meta absorbing local discovery, audience growth stalled for years -- so the gap is NOT clean single-name alpha; a meaningful slice is shared secular de-rating of standalone local-search platforms (TRIP screens cheap for the same AI/Google-encroachment reason). Treat ~half the apparent discount as sector beta on the AI-displacement factor, not idiosyncratic edge. interrogator_dossier:
# Yelp Inc. (YELP) — Interrogator Forensic Dossier

*Six consecutive earnings calls reviewed: Q1 2025 (2025-05-08) through Q1 2026 (2026-05-08). Financials from the screener scan (FY2021–FY2025 trajectory) plus TTM/latest-quarter overrides as of 2026-03-31.*

## 1. NARRATIVE ARC

The story management tells has quietly but decisively shifted from **"resilient two-sided local-ad platform"** to **"AI transformation that will reaccelerate a structurally stalling core."** That shift is the whole dossier.

- **Q1 2025** opened confidently: "8% year-over-year revenue growth and strong profitability," Services "16th consecutive quarter of double-digit growth," RR&O (Restaurants, Retail & Other) merely "challenging." AI was a feature layer (Yelp Assistant, photo recognition, answering services "coming soon"). The frame was *growth with a soft pocket*.
- By **Q2 2025** the soft pocket widened: revenue growth decelerated to **4%**, RR&O **-5%**, and even Services growth halved from 14% to **8%**. Management introduced the **data-licensing / AI-search API** narrative ("API calls up 20x over the past year… $10M annual run rate") — the first new growth driver bolted on to offset core deceleration.
- **Q3 2025**: revenue +4% again, RR&O -2%, Services +7%, ad clicks now **-11% YoY**. The DoorDash partnership (food ordering) and the Yelp Host/Receptionist voice-SaaS products are introduced. Adjusted EBITDA actually *declined* 3% YoY for the first time in the window.
- **Q4/FY2025**: the pivot is now explicit and capitalized into a three-pillar 2026 strategy — (1) conversational Yelp Assistant, (2) AI SaaS for businesses (Hatch acquisition closed, Yelp Host), (3) data licensing across the AI ecosystem (OpenAI deal signed). RR&O revenue fell **-6%** for the full year.
- **Q1 2026** is the tell: total revenue **+1% YoY**, RR&O ad revenue **-11%**, Services ad revenue **+1%** (services has now effectively stalled too), paying locations **-6% to 485k**, net income **-27% YoY**, adjusted EBITDA **-7%**. The *only* thing growing is **Other revenue +75%** (Hatch inclusion + licensing + food ordering). Management reframes the entire business around a **$250M other-revenue run-rate target by end-2028** (vs ~$116M run-rate in Q1-26).

**Growth drivers introduced then leaned on harder each quarter:** Yelp Assistant (project share 5%→15%), data licensing (OpenAI, Apple Maps, Bing, Alexa, Meta.ai), Yelp Host (1.5M-call annual run-rate by April 2026), Hatch ($34M ARR, +92%). **Quietly contracting:** the core advertising engine — paying locations have fallen every single quarter (517k→515k→512k→…→485k), ad clicks have been negative all six quarters and are accelerating downward (-3%→-7%→-11%→-10%), and the headline revenue growth rate collapsed from 8% to 1% across five quarters. The narrative has gotten *richer* (more product names, bigger TAMs) precisely as the financial base got *poorer*. That is the classic shape of a management team out-talking a decelerating core.

## 2. CLAIMS vs FINANCIALS (forensic)

| Mgmt claim | Quarter | Hard number | Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|
| "Services… 16th consecutive quarter of double-digit growth" | Q1-25 | Services +14%, but +8% by Q2-25, +7% Q3-25, **+1% Q1-26** | **Diverges** — the growth engine the whole thesis rested on decelerated to a stall in 12 months |
| "strong profitability… margin potential" | all | Net margin 7%→12%→10%→5%; FY25 NI +10%; **Q1-26 NI -27% YoY** | **Mixed** — FY-level profitability real, but the latest quarter shows margin compression as they spend into the pivot |
| "record net revenue" | Q2/Q3-25 | True quarter-on-quarter, but FY25 rev only **+4%** and Q1-26 **+1%** | **Technically true, narratively misleading** — "records" mask a 1% growth rate |
| "disciplined expense management… flat headcount" | all | FCF margin 22%, TTM FCF $281M, P/FCF 4.7x | **Matches** — this is the genuinely strong part; the cash engine is real |
| Aggressive buyback / shrinking share count | Q4-25 | Diluted shares 86M→67M (-22%) 2021–2025; $292M repurchased FY25; **$125M in Q1-26 at $24.58** | **Matches and is the strongest pro-shareholder fact in the file** |
| "other revenue… $250M run-rate by 2028" | Q1-26 | Other rev +75% to $29M/qtr (~$116M run-rate); needs ~2.2x in <3 yrs | **Aspirational** — credible given Hatch's $34M ARR +92% and licensing, but it is replacing, not adding to, a shrinking ad base |
| EPS growth +45% 5-yr CAGR | scan | FY EPS 0.50→0.50→1.35→1.88→2.24 | **Matches, but flatters** — most of the EPS CAGR is buyback-driven share-count reduction + margin expansion off a low 2021/22 base, not revenue growth (rev CAGR only +7%, decelerating to +1%) |

**Net read:** the cash-generation and capital-return claims are fully corroborated by the numbers — this is a real FCF machine buying back a fifth of itself. But the *growth* narrative is materially richer than the trajectory justifies: every forward-looking driver management emphasizes (AI search, voice SaaS, Hatch) is being marshalled to explain why a +1% top line will someday reaccelerate, while the core ad business shrinks on paying-location count, clicks, and now even Services.

## 3. TONE & CONFIDENCE TRAJECTORY

**Trajectory: Declining (on the core) / Pivoting (on the narrative).**

- **Q1-25 (offensive):** "continued strength," "strong momentum," "excited by the opportunities." Confident, growth-forward.
- **Q2-25 (first defensive notes):** "advertisers exercised increased caution," "did not see the seasonal increase we would typically expect," macro invoked repeatedly. Tone tilts to explaining absence of growth.
- **Q3-25 (defensive + reframing):** "heightened macroeconomic uncertainties will persist," EBITDA *down* 3%, but heavy pivot language — "Yelp's transformation with AI continues to accelerate," 35 new features. Offense rhetorically, defense financially.
- **Q4-25 (full pivot):** the word "transform"/"transformation" dominates; "we plan to increase our investments in 2026." Management is now selling a *future* business.
- **Q1-26 (defensive core, aggressive pivot):** "local businesses have continued to face a challenging economic environment," NI -27%, and the new framing device — a 2028 other-revenue target — to redirect attention away from a +1% quarter and a -11% RR&O. Note the late mention of "the conflict in the Middle East" / "dynamics… in March" as a fresh macro excuse.

The CPC mechanic is a recurring rhetorical shield: clicks fall double-digits, CPC rises double-digits, and management frames this as "delivering fewer but higher-quality clicks" and "advertiser demand outpacing consumer demand." That is partly true (services mix), but it is also how a platform whose *consumer engagement is shrinking* (app unique devices -2% YoY, "consumers visited restaurants with reduced frequency") describes monetizing a declining audience harder.

## 4. GUIDANCE CREDIBILITY

**Reliability: High (on hitting/beating the printed guide) — but the guide itself has been serially lowered.**

- The screener records **7/7 quarterly beats (100%)**, and the calls corroborate a consistent beat-and-raise *cadence* on the bottom line: Q1-25 EBITDA "$15M above the high end," Q2-25 "$11M above," Q3-25 "$13M above," Q1-26 EBITDA "$15M above the high end." Management explicitly flows outperformance to EBITDA — David Schwarzbach, Q1-26: "as we've been able to outperform our guidance, we've flowed through that incremental revenue to EBITDA."
- BUT the **full-year revenue guide was cut through the year**: FY25 revenue guided to $1.465–1.485B (Q1-25) → narrowed to $1.465–1.475B (Q2) → *lowered* to $1.460–1.465B (Q3). They beat the *lowered* bar. That is sandbag-and-beat on a declining trajectory, not genuine upside.
- So: **High reliability on the printed number, Medium on the trajectory.** They reliably clear a bar they keep setting lower. The EBITDA beats are real and partly mechanical (capitalized software development was explicitly cited as a Q1-26 EBITDA tailwind — a quality-of-earnings caveat).

## 5. ANALYST PRESSURE POINTS

- **"What needs to change for RR&O to stabilize?"** (KeyBanc, Raymond James, Baird recurring). Management's answer never changes: it's "macro," in-restaurant dining "will return," they're "well positioned." Two-plus years of the same answer while RR&O goes from -3% to -11%. The non-answer is the answer: they have no lever on the structural side.
- **OpenAI / data-licensing visibility** (BofA, Q1-26, pointed): "when I search for restaurants on ChatGPT today, Yelp content appears relatively limited versus Reddit, OpenTable, Tripadvisor… what needs to happen for Yelp to become more visible?" Stoppelman *cannot answer* — "we can't really comment on that nor do we have all the details of their plans." The load-bearing AI-distribution story is outside Yelp's control and management admits it.
- **Hatch margin / contribution** (multiple): management deflects to "growth not margin is the focus," and flags that retention payments are being *added back* to adjusted EBITDA — a real quality-of-earnings caution analysts surfaced.
- **Yelp Assistant → actual paid-lead conversion / ROI** (Baird, Q1-26): asked directly whether assistant-driven projects convert and lift advertiser ROI materially differently; answer was qualitative ("we feel really good," "early signs are really good"), no conversion metric. The engagement stat (15% of RaQ projects) is an activity metric, not a revenue metric.

The throughline: analysts keep probing whether the AI/other-revenue story is *incremental and monetizable* or merely *replacing* a shrinking ad base, and management's answers are consistently directional and TAM-flavored rather than quantified.

## 6. RED FLAGS / GREEN FLAGS

🔴 **Paying advertising locations down every quarter** — 517k → 515k → 512k → … → **485k** (Q1-26, -6% YoY). The customer base of the core business is eroding, not just its spend.
🔴 **Services has now stalled** (+1% Q1-26 ad rev) — the one durable growth engine that justified the multiple has joined RR&O in deceleration.
🔴 **App unique devices -2% YoY**, consumers visiting "with reduced frequency" — the consumer flywheel (reviews → traffic → ad value) is going backwards.
🔴 **Net income -27% and adjusted EBITDA -7% in Q1-26** — the pivot is now visibly costing current profitability.
🔴 **Insider open-market activity: 0 buys vs 33 sells (476,663 sh)** — zero real conviction from people who know best; the scan's positive buy_ratio is grant-inflated and false.
🔴 **AI-distribution thesis is outside Yelp's control** — management admits it can't comment on how OpenAI surfaces its content; Reddit/OpenTable/Tripadvisor are ahead in ChatGPT today.
🟢 **Prodigious, opportunistic buyback** — 86M→67M shares (-22%) over four years; $125M in Q1-26 at **$24.58** (near lows), $414M authorization remaining. Management is buying aggressively into weakness, the most credible bull signal in the file.
🟢 **Genuine cash machine** — 90% gross margin, 22% FCF margin, TTM FCF $281M, P/FCF 4.7x, net debt only ~$45M. The balance sheet is clean and the cash is real.
🟢 **Other revenue +75%, Hatch $34M ARR +92%, food ordering +88%** — the new engines are growing fast off a small base; the pivot has *some* traction, not zero.
🟢 **SBC discipline** — SBC/revenue cut to 8% (target <6% by 2027), improving GAAP earnings quality over time.

## 7. HIDDEN SIGNALS

- **Terminology migration:** "advertising platform" / "local business" language (2025) gives way to "AI transformation," "conversational," "answers and actions," "essential partner in the AI ecosystem" (2026). When a company restocks its vocabulary faster than its revenue grows, the new words are doing the work the numbers can't.
- **Capital allocation language softened on M&A:** "retaining balance sheet capacity for *potential* acquisitions" (2025) became an actual $270–300M cash deal for Hatch (Feb 2026) — they spent the optionality. The buyback line item also reordered: in Q1-26 capital-allocation priorities are listed as "strategic transactions, traffic acquisition, *then* share repurchases" — buyback dropped to third, a subtle signal they intend to spend more on the pivot and less on the float.
- **"Capitalized software development" repeatedly cited as an EBITDA/margin tailwind** (Q3-25, Q1-26) — more greenfield AI projects → more capitalized cost → flattered adjusted EBITDA. A genuine investment, but it inflates the headline margin the bull case leans on.
- **CFO's 5-year revenue-mix answer** (Q4-25): explicitly guiding investors to expect the mix to shift *away* from ads toward SaaS/licensing/transaction — i.e., management itself is signaling the ad business is not the future growth driver.
- **TCS Capital activism is dormant, not live:** the 2023 sale-push has produced no process; management's own buyback is the de facto "activist" return-of-capital, not a third-party catalyst.

## 8. CAPITAL-ALLOCATION VERDICT (for the director)

**Classification: PIVOTING** (with a STABLE-to-deteriorating core cash engine underneath).

This is a high-FCF, deeply-bought-back local-ad platform whose core advertising business is in slow structural decline (paying locations, clicks, and now even Services all negative), executing a credible-but-unproven pivot into AI SaaS (Hatch, Yelp Host) and data licensing to reaccelerate. The capital allocation itself is shareholder-friendly and disciplined — the 22% share-count reduction at low prices is the real value-creation lever and puts a floor under per-share value even if revenue merely stabilizes.

**The single most important thing the director must weigh before sizing:** *Is the core ad business stabilizing at a lower volume (in which case 4.7x FCF + a 22%-and-shrinking share count is a genuine fear-print and the buyback alone re-rates it), or is it in terminal secular decline as Google Maps / Meta / AI-search absorb local discovery (in which case the cheapness is a value trap and "other revenue" is merely treading water against a melting ad base)?* Q1-26 — Services stalling to +1% and consumer devices -2% — tilts the evidence toward the latter being a live risk, not a tail.

**ECONOMIC MOAT:** intangible-brand + network (330M reviews, 8M listings, 500M photos — a genuine first-party local-content asset that AI players are now licensing). But the moat is **ERODING**, not wide: ROIC 9.0% (barely above WACC) and falling, paying-location count declining, audience shrinking, and CPC-up/clicks-down monetization is the signature of harvesting a declining base rather than compounding a growing one. The content corpus has real scarcity value (the OpenAI/Apple/Bing licensing deals prove third parties will pay for it), which is why the moat is eroding rather than gone.

**SECULAR THREAT — material, bordering terminal:** **AI-displacement of local discovery.** Google Maps and Meta already absorb the top of the local-discovery funnel; now generative-AI search (ChatGPT, Gemini) threatens to disintermediate Yelp's consumer destination entirely — and management *admits* Yelp content is currently a minor source in ChatGPT vs Reddit/OpenTable/Tripadvisor. Yelp's counter (license the data to the disruptors) monetizes the corpus but cedes the consumer relationship and caps it at a licensing fee, not an ad-network rent. This is a structurally-shrinking-base risk on the *ad* segment, partially offset by the licensing/SaaS optionality and the buyback. The cheapness is therefore a **hybrid**: a real fear-print on a cash machine with a floor, sitting on a genuinely melting consumer-ad franchise — value, but value-trap-adjacent, which caps the value conviction.

CREDIBILITY_SCORE: 3 | TRAJECTORY: PIVOTING | MOAT: ERODING | MOAT_TREND: ERODING | SECULAR_THREAT: material

bull_thesis: Yelp is a cash machine priced as if the business is in terminal decline. It earns roughly 90 cents of gross profit on every revenue dollar, converts about 22% of revenue into free cash flow (the cash left after running and reinvesting in the business), and trades at about 5 times that free cash flow and about 1 times EV/sales (enterprise value divided by annual revenue). Despite the gloom, it still generated about $281 million of free cash flow over the trailing twelve months and has beaten its own guidance in all 7 of its recent quarters. The case rests on four legs. First, the cash being returned to shareholders is outrunning the erosion of the core business: the share count has been cut 22% (from 86 million to 67 million between 2021 and 2025), a fresh $500 million buyback was authorized in February 2026 with $414 million left (about 28% of all shares outstanding), and management has been buying aggressively into the weakness (an average price of $24.58 in the first quarter of 2026). On $281 million of free cash flow, Yelp is retiring more than 4-5% of its shares a year, so even a flat advertising base lifts per-share value over time (full-year 2025 earnings per share rose 19% on net income up 10%; the difference is the buyback). Second, diversification is real: 'other revenue' (Hatch, an AI lead-management tool, up 92%; DoorDash food ordering, up 88%; and AI data licensing to partners including OpenAI, Apple, Bing, and Meta.ai) grew 75% year over year to $29 million a quarter, with a target of a $250 million annual run-rate by the end of 2028 at software-like margins, and the market is paying roughly nothing for it today. Third, there is an AI-licensing opportunity: Yelp's 330 million reviews and 8 million business listings are scarce, trusted local content that AI search engines need (API calls are up 20 times year over year). Fourth, there is a litigation opportunity: Yelp's antitrust suit against Google survived its motion to dismiss in October 2025, with all claims proceeding, giving a free option on damages or court-ordered relief. If the advertising base declines no faster than the mid-single digits, the buyback continues, and 'other revenue' scales, a re-rating toward marketplace peers (Tripadvisor at roughly 12-15 times, Angi at roughly 20 times free cash flow) would imply the stock could double.
bear_thesis: Yelp is cheap for a reason: the market is correctly discounting a shrinking local-search business that AI is steadily cutting out of the loop, not a bargain the market has missed. The decline of the existing customer base is hidden under the headline numbers. Paying advertising locations fell 6% year over year to 485,000 (down from 517,000), ad clicks fell 10-11% year over year, and the 'resilient' Services advertising leg collapsed from 14% growth in the first quarter of 2025 to just 1% in the first quarter of 2026 -- and that happened in a non-recessionary economy, which points to structural loss of search intent, not a temporary dip in the Restaurants, Retail and Other (RR&O) segment. RR&O advertising revenue fell 6% for full-year 2025 and the decline accelerated to 11% in the first quarter of 2026. Revenue only looks stable because the cost per click rose 8-14% year over year even as clicks fell -- scarcity pricing that management itself acknowledged ('fewer clicks resulted in higher prices,' third quarter of 2025). The underlying threat is AI displacement: Google's AI Overviews and ChatGPT answer 'best restaurant near me' inside the result page, eliminating the click that Yelp gets paid for; the app's unique devices are already down 2% year over year. The strategic answer -- licensing Yelp's data to the very companies doing the displacing -- trades a high-margin advertising dollar for a low-margin wholesale penny. When asked why Yelp's content looked 'relatively limited versus Reddit, OpenTable, and Tripadvisor' on ChatGPT (Bansal, first quarter of 2026), the CEO had no answer ('we can't comment'). The potential catalysts are weak: the antitrust suit has no court date, will take years, is a single yes-or-no outcome with no damages figure attached; the OpenAI licensing arrangement is controlled by a third party and not quantified; and the 2023 push by activist investor TCS Capital to force a sale already failed and the investor exited. Profitability is reversing -- adjusted EBITDA margin fell from 27% to 22%, and adjusted EBITDA was down 7% and net income down 27% year over year in the first quarter of 2026 -- the moment they started spending behind Hatch. The balance sheet flipped from net cash to net debt of $45 million after the Hatch deal, which means the 'fortress balance sheet,' 'net cash,' and '138% margin of safety' figures behind the bull case are out of date. Insiders sold 476,663 shares with zero open-market purchases. The confirming evidence would be another quarter of falling locations plus Services growth at zero or below plus 'other revenue' missing its ramp -- which together would make this a value trap where the buyback only slows the bleed.
consensus_delta: For a deep-value name, the question is whether the market is pricing terminal decline into a company whose earnings power is actually stable. Here the market (a Hold consensus, a roughly $29 price target, only about 20% upside, and analyst targets down 11% versus a year ago) is roughly right -- it is the screener's own value inputs that are wrong. The bullish claim of a mispricing ('138% margin of safety, net cash, a discounted-cash-flow value of $68') leans on stale figures: the net-cash position has flipped to net debt of $45 million after the Hatch deal, and the discounted-cash-flow estimate assumes a durable advertising base that is in fact shrinking on a same-store basis (paying locations down 6%, Services growth down from 14% to 1%, clicks down 10%). Where we genuinely disagree with the market is narrower and cuts both ways: the market under-credits how much the buyback compounds per-share value and the free option in the Yelp-versus-Google lawsuit, but it correctly prices the structural loss of search intent that management keeps mislabeling as 'macro.' The assumption that is factually wrong is not the market's -- it is the screener's premise that Services is a resilient growth engine, which the first quarter of 2026 (just 1% growth) refutes. In short, there is not enough hidden upside here to override an eroding base.
forcing_function: There is no fixed, binding event that forces this stock to re-rate. The candidate events are all soft or have no date attached. First, the Yelp-versus-Google antitrust suit survived its motion to dismiss in October 2025, with all claims proceeding, but it is in early discovery with no trial date and no damages figure (a single yes-or-no outcome, years away). Second, the scale-up of OpenAI and other data licensing is controlled by third parties, is not quantified, and management 'can't comment' on whether Yelp becomes a primary source. Third, the target of a $250 million 'other revenue' annual run-rate by the end of 2028 is a soft milestone two and a half years out, not an event. Fourth, the earlier activist push by TCS Capital to force a sale has already played out (2023, no deal, the investor exited). The only dated checkpoints are the quarterly results (the second quarter of 2026, around August, and the third quarter, around November), and those are more likely to confirm the erosion than to force the stock higher. By the standards of the current market regime, this is exactly the kind of thesis to deprioritize: it depends on one shared market force (AI search displacing Yelp) with no independent driver that would resolve it on its own.
valley_of_death: The risky window is the next 3-9 months (the second-quarter 2026 report in early August and the third-quarter report in early November). The most likely path is continued deterioration that re-rates the stock down before any bullish catalyst can fire. Expect another quarter of falling paying locations (485,000 and trending lower), Services advertising growth at or near zero, RR&O advertising revenue down by double digits, and adjusted EBITDA margin stuck near 22% (versus 25-27% in 2025) as spending on Hatch and on acquiring traffic runs ahead of the revenue it brings in. There is no wall of maturing debt to worry about (net debt is only $45 million, with ample liquidity) and free cash flow stays positive -- so the risk is not insolvency, it is a cut to guidance or a reset of margins that compresses the valuation further. Management has already flagged the year ahead: 'we expect the operating environment for local businesses to remain challenging this year.' The buyback cushions the blow but cannot reverse a decline in same-store revenue if Services growth turns negative.
positioning_washout: There is little chance of forced selling here. Yelp pays no dividend, so there is no income-focused shareholder base that a cut could force to sell, and at a market value of about $1.45 billion it is not a candidate to be dropped from a major index. The visible flow is insider selling (33 sales totaling 476,663 shares, with zero open-market purchases) and the company's own steady buyback soaking up shares. There is no forced-selling event to wait for and no crowded position that needs to unwind; the ownership is ordinary institutional and index money. That removes the 'wait for a wash-out' setup -- there is no identifiable flush that would reset the risk and reward -- which pushes the call toward passing for now rather than keeping it on a watchlist.
moderator_conclusion: Pass for now, with low conviction (a 2 on our 5-point scale). Yelp is a high-quality cash engine -- a 90% gross margin, about $281 million of trailing-twelve-month free cash flow, a 22% reduction in share count, and 7 beats in 7 quarters -- wrapped around a structurally eroding local-advertising business that AI search is steadily cutting out of the loop. The forensic case is damaging precisely where it matters most, on the large current earnings base: Services growth collapsed from 14% to 1% in a non-recessionary economy, paying locations are down 6%, clicks are down 10%, app devices are down 2%, RR&O is down 11% and accelerating, margins are reversing (from 27% to 22%), and the screener's headline safety figures (net cash, a 138% margin of safety, a discounted-cash-flow value of $68) rest on stale inputs that the live balance sheet (net debt of $45 million after the Hatch deal) and the trajectory contradict. Valuing each business line separately (a sum-of-the-parts approach) gives a current fair value of about $33 (a range of $30-36), well below the screener's $54-68, against a $22.88 price -- so there is a real discount, but it is mostly shared sector pressure (Tripadvisor, Angi, and Yelp all re-rate down on the same Google and AI-search threat), not an advantage unique to Yelp. The catalysts are all soft, undated, or already spent: the antitrust suit is undated single-outcome litigation, the OpenAI and licensing lever is controlled by a third party and unquantified, and the activist push already failed in 2023. The current market regime specifically penalizes theses with no dated catalyst and those that hinge on one dominant shared force -- and AI investment and AI displacement is the very force it flags -- while favoring structural special situations among distressed or forced sellers, which this is not. With an eroding competitive advantage, a material (bordering on terminal) AI-displacement threat, no forced-selling wash-out to wait for, and no dated trigger to force a re-rating, there is no entry edge here. Separately, judged on valuation alone (setting the regime and catalyst questions aside), the stock is cheap against that roughly $33 sum-of-the-parts value, and the free cash flow and buyback are real -- but the eroding competitive advantage and the material secular threat cap that standalone conviction at a 3, because a low multiple on a structurally shrinking, AI-disintermediated base is a value-trap risk, not durable value. The buyback buys time, not immunity.
sop_breakdown: This is a probability-weighted base case (roughly 55% weight on the bear view, 45% on the bull), plus a modest credit for the buyback's compounding and a thin credit for the litigation option. Valuing each business line separately: Services advertising of $948 million at 1.6 times sales is worth about $1,517 million; RR&O advertising of $444 million at 0.85 times sales is worth about $377 million; other revenue at a $116 million annual run-rate at 3.0 times sales is worth about $348 million -- giving a gross enterprise value of $2,242 million. Subtract net debt of $45 million and add the risk-adjusted value of the Yelp-versus-Google option of about $60 million, for roughly $2,257 million of equity, or about $35.8 per share across about 63 million shares. A free-cash-flow cross-check (7 times trailing-twelve-month free cash flow of $281 million, or $1,967 million, less $45 million of net debt) gives about $30.5 per share. Blending the two lands at about $33. Note that the screener's discounted-cash-flow value of $68, its Buffett-style value of $54, and its net-cash inputs are stale (they assume net cash that is now net debt of $45 million after the Hatch deal, and a Services base that is in fact declining), so they overstate fair value -- the live sum-of-the-parts lands well below them.
risk_reward: On a structural read, the asymmetry tilts to the downside. At $22.88 the price already sits below the bear-case parts value of $25.3. The downside to where the thesis breaks (Services and RR&O valued as run-off businesses as AI-search displacement accelerates) is about $17-18, or roughly 22% lower. The upside to the $33 base case is about 44%, and to the $50 bull case about 118%. That optical reward-to-risk of better than 1.5 to 1 against the base case is undermined by an eroding competitive advantage and the lack of any dated catalyst -- and realizing the upside requires the AI-displacement thesis to be wrong, which is the one shared market force the current regime says to avoid concentrating in.
peer_comps_note: On the peer comparison, Yelp's 4.6 times free cash flow is far below Tripadvisor (roughly 12-15 times), Angi (roughly 20 times where profitable), and Google, and it pairs that with a best-in-class 90% gross margin -- so on the surface it looks like deep value. But the discount is shared sector pressure, not an advantage unique to Yelp: Tripadvisor, Angi, and Yelp all re-rate down together on the same unresolved force -- Google and AI search cutting into the consumer review-and-discovery business they all monetize. Crediting the full gap as a Yelp-specific edge would be a mistake; much of it is the whole group repricing the AI-displacement threat. The peer data itself concedes that the 'core risk is Google's AI Overviews and Maps eating local search intent -- a secular threat that justifies some discount.' Our live sum-of-the-parts value of $33 anchors the multiple at a structurally aware 1-1.6 times EV/sales (enterprise value divided by annual revenue), not at the premiums of Tripadvisor or Angi.
interrogator_dossier:
# Yelp Inc. -- A Deep Dive on Management and the Setup

*Six consecutive earnings calls reviewed: the first quarter of 2025 (2025-05-08) through the first quarter of 2026 (2026-05-08). Financials cross-referenced against the screener trajectory (full-year 2021 through full-year 2025) plus the trailing-twelve-month and latest-quarter overrides (as of 2026-03-31).*

---

## 1. The story over time

The story management told a year ago and the story it tells today are materially different -- not in branding, but in what is actually carrying the business.

**First quarter of 2025 (the high-water mark of the old story):** Yelp opened the period reporting 8% year-over-year revenue growth and an expanding margin, led by Services (up 14% year over year, the "16th consecutive quarter of double-digit growth"). The story had two legs: a resilient, high-margin Services advertising engine offsetting a weak Restaurants, Retail and Other (RR&O) book, and AI as an enhancement layer -- Yelp Assistant driving Request-a-Quote project volume, themed ad formats, and better matching. RR&O was a known sore spot (down 3% year over year) but was framed as cyclical, not structural.

**The deceleration is the spine of the story.** Watch the headline revenue growth degrade call by call: up 8% in the first quarter of 2025, then up 4% in the second quarter, up 4% in the third quarter, up 4% for the full year, and up just 1% in the first quarter of 2026. Services advertising, the supposed durable leg, decelerated in lockstep: up 14% (first quarter of 2025), up 8% (second quarter), up 7% (third quarter), up 1% (first quarter of 2026). That last number is the tell -- the "resilient" growth engine has converged to flat. RR&O never stopped bleeding: down 3%, then down 5%, then down 2% (down 6% for the full year), then down 11% in the first quarter of 2026 -- accelerating downward, not stabilizing.

**The pivot in the narrative:** as the advertising business flattened, management progressively re-pointed the story at three new pillars introduced and amplified across the year -- AI software products (Yelp Host call-answering for restaurants, Yelp Receptionist for services, now folded into the Hatch acquisition closed in February 2026), data licensing to AI search players (the "$10 million run-rate" disclosed in the second quarter of 2025, then the OpenAI agreement headlined in the fourth quarter of 2025 and the first quarter of 2026), and transaction revenue via the DoorDash food-ordering partnership. By the first quarter of 2026 these are bundled as "other revenue," which grew 75% year over year to $29 million -- and management has planted a flag: a $250 million annual run-rate for other revenue by the end of 2028, "more than double" the first-quarter 2026 run-rate.

So the arc is a classic incumbent in transition: the core way it makes money (cost-per-click advertising on local-business listings) is decelerating toward zero growth, and a small, fast-growing, lower-visibility set of adjacent businesses is being promoted to carry the equity story. The question the rest of this dossier interrogates: is the new leg real and large enough, or is it a $29 million tail being asked to do the work of a $1.4 billion body that is quietly shrinking on a same-store basis?

---

## 2. Claims versus the financials

| Management claim | Where stated | The hard numbers | Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|
| "Record net revenue, strong profitability" | Second and third quarters of 2025, full year 2025 | Full-year 2025 revenue $1.46 billion (up 4%), net income $146 million (up 10%), earnings per share $2.24 (up 19%) | True but flattering. Record revenue is real; but 4% is the slowest growth in the five-year table (revenue had compounded at 7.1% a year). Earnings-per-share growth is driven by the buyback, not by operations. |
| Services is the durable growth engine | every call | Services advertising revenue up 14%, then 8%, then 7%, then 1% (first quarter of 2026) | Decisively breaking. The "resilient" leg decelerated 14 points in four quarters to flat. The thesis pillar is failing in real time. |
| The business has 24-27% adjusted-EBITDA margin potential | first through third quarters of 2025 | Adjusted EBITDA margin 24%, then 27%, then 26% in 2025, but 22% in the first quarter of 2026; adjusted EBITDA down 7% year over year, net income down 27% year over year in the first quarter of 2026 | Reversing. The margin story peaked in mid-2025 and is now contracting as they spend behind Hatch and on acquiring traffic. |
| Earnings-per-share growth is strong (up 19%) | full year 2025 | Diluted shares cut from 86 million to 67 million (down 22%) between 2021 and 2025; full-year 2025 earnings per share up 19% | Engineered, not earned. Net income up 10% but earnings per share up 19% -- the gap is the buyback. Trailing-twelve-month diluted earnings per share has already rolled to $2.18 (below the $2.24 full-year print), and the latest-quarter earnings per share was down 17% year over year. The per-share tailwind is decelerating as the price (and so the number of shares retired per dollar) matters less than the shrinking earnings being divided. |
| Rising cost per click shows pricing power | second and third quarters of 2025, first quarter of 2026 | Cost per click up 9%, then 11%, then 14%, then 8% year over year; ad clicks down 3%, then 7%, then 11%, then 10% year over year | A warning sign dressed up as strength. Rising cost per click on falling clicks is mix and scarcity, not demand. Management itself admits (third quarter of 2025, Schwarzbach) that "fewer clicks on the platform resulted in higher prices." Revenue per click is propping up a platform with shrinking engagement. |
| Free cash flow generation is strong (4.6 times free cash flow) | implied by the screener | Free-cash-flow margin 22%; trailing-twelve-month free cash flow $281 million; net debt now $45 million (was net cash) | Free cash flow is real, but the balance sheet flipped. The screener's "net cash of $299 million" is stale. After the Hatch deal ($271 million cash), the recomputed 2026-03-31 position is net debt of $45 million (total debt $155 million less cash $110 million). The "fortress balance sheet" leg of the value case is materially weaker than the input headline. |
| Other revenue is the future ($250 million by 2028) | first quarter of 2026 | Other revenue $29 million in the first quarter of 2026 (about $116 million annualized run-rate); up 75% year over year | Real growth, trivial base. Even hitting a $250 million run-rate by the end of 2028 is about $130 million of incremental revenue spread over about three years against a $1.46 billion base that is flat to down. It does not offset a shrinking advertising core on any near-term horizon. |
